segments.text: Part 5: Nitrogen Metabolism

How Do the Various Organic Forms of Nitrogen Arise?
Nitrogen in organic compounds is predominantly in a reduced state
But the prevalent forms of nitrogen in the environment are inorganic and oxidized:
N 2 (dinitrogen gas)
NO 3 - (nitrate anion)
The two principal routes for nitrogen acquisition are nitrate assimilation and nitrogen fixation
Both these lead to formation of ammonium ions
Reactions that incorporate ammonium ion into organic molecules follow

25.1 Which Metabolic Pathways Allow Organisms to Live on Inorganic Forms of Nitrogen?
Nitrogen is cycled between organisms and the inanimate environment
Nitrate assimilation – the reduction of nitrate to NH 4 + in plants, various fungi, and certain bacteria, in a two-step metabolic pathway
Nitrogen fixation – the formation of NH 4 + from N 2 gas
Organisms release nitrogen either as NH 4 + or as organic compounds such as urea
Bacteria return these to the environment by oxidizing them

Nitrate Assimilation
The two-electron reduction of nitrate to nitrite, catalyzed by nitrate reductase
The six-electron reduction of nitrite to ammonium, catalyzed by nitrite reductase
Nitrate assimilation is the predominant means by which green plants, algae, and many microorganisms acquire nitrogen
Nitrate assimilation accounts for more than 99% of the inorganic nitrogen assimilated into organisms
Nitrate (fertilizer/manure) comes from the breakdown of organic matter in the soil

Nitrogen fixation (N 2 from thin air)
Soybean plants can meet their nitrogen requirements both by assimilating nitrate and, in symbiosis with bacteria, fixing N 2 .

Nitrogen Fixation
Only occurs in certain prokaryotes
Rhizobia fix nitrogen in symbiotic association with leguminous plants
Rhizobia fix N for the plant and the plant provides Rhizobia with carbon substrates
Nitrogen-fixing systems require:
nitrogenase , ATP, O 2 -free conditions
Regulatory controls:
ADP inhibits nitrogenase
NH 4 + inhibits expression of nif genes

Nitrogen Fixation
Nitrogen-fixing by Nitrogenase
Process is spontaneous but has a very high activation energy
Reduction of one Nitrogen molecule to 2 ammonia molecules requires 12 ATPs
Once ammonia is produced, it can be incorporated into organic molecules

Amino acid synthesis
In humans, essential amino acids must be obtained from the diet
Essential amino acids
Leu , Ile, Val, Phe , Trp , Thr , Met, Lys
Arg & His: during development
Non-essential amino acids can be synthesized by transamination of α- keto acid with glutamate

Amino Groups for Amino Acids Are Derived From Glutamate in Transamination Reactions
Figure 25.19 Glutamate-dependent transamination of α - keto acid carbon skeletons is a primary mechanism for amino acid synthesis.
This reaction requires pyridoxal phosphate (PLP) which comes from vitamin B6

Famous keto acids
Pyruvate is the α- keto acid of Alanine
α- ketoglutarate is the α- keto acid of glutamate
Oxaloacetate is the α- keto acid of aspartate

Reductive amination
Ammonia can be used to make an amino acid from an α- keto acid through Reductive amination
Most prominently: α- ketoglutarate to glutamate by glutamate dehydrogenase
Process can go in reverse (oxidative deamination) to remove N from amino acid

Reductive amination
Glutamate dehydrogenase
reductive amination of α - ketoglutarate to form glutamate
Glutamate can be aminated a second time to make glutamine
Reaction requires NADPH

Nitrogen Excretion
Urea cycle is the process by which excess Nitrogen is converted into Urea for excretion by the kidneys
2 ways to remove amino group = two sources for Nitrogen in Urea cycle
Transamination:
Final product is Aspartate
Oxidative deamination:
Direct removal of ammonia (NH4+)

Transamination
Amino group is removed to make keto -acid
Received by a - ketoglutarate to make glutamate
Glutamate is converted to Aspartate
a - ketoglutarate is regenerated

Carbamoyl P synthetase I: activated by N- acetylglutamate
High protein diet = high glutamate in mitochondria = high N- acetylglutamate
Fumarate is a product of the UC, can be used in the TCA cycle

Carbamoyl -Phosphate Synthetase I
Reaction is:
NH 4 + + HCO 3 - + 2ATP  →
H 2 N-COO-PO 3 2- + 2ADP + P i + 2H +
This reaction is an early step in the urea cycle
Carbamoylphosphate

Essentially we have synthesized Arginine
The R-group of Arg is a guanidinium
Hydrolysis of guanidinium group releases urea, ornithine is recycled
Each urea = 2 amines
1 from ammonia
1 from aspartate

Urea Cycle
Deficiency in UC enzymes: Hyperammonemia
Intellectual disability, seizures, coma, death
(Ammonia toxicity)

Serotonin and Melatonin

25.5 How Does Amino Acid Catabolism Lead into Pathways of Energy Production
The 20 common amino acids are degraded by 20 different pathways to produce just 7 metabolic intermediates
glucogenic amino acids: can be used to produce glucose
ketogenic amino acids: can be used to produce ketone bodies
Most amino acids are both glucogenic and ketogenic
Only Leu and Lys are purely ketogenic

Figure 25.41 Metabolic degradation of the common amino acids. Glucogenic amino acids are shown in pink, ketogenic in blue.
Ketone bodies
